claim_under_attack: Meta should keep Muse frontier models closed in the near term because the company currently needs product control, rollout speed, and monetization leverage more than open-ecosystem signaling.
evidence_or_reason: Side B treats 'product control' and 'monetization leverage' as self-evidently served by a closed model, but supplies no mechanism showing how closure actually accelerates either. Meta's owned surfaces (WhatsApp, Instagram, Facebook, Messenger) are already distribution-locked — users cannot take their social graph elsewhere regardless of model licensing. That means the marginal value of model closure for rollout speed is near zero: Meta can ship Muse integrations just as fast with open weights because competitor access to the weights does not reduce Meta's surface control. On monetization, Side B assumes closure enables premium pricing or bundling, but the case packet describes only 'private-preview API access to selected partners,' not a consumer subscription tier or API revenue line. Until there is evidence that Muse Spark's closed status is the gating variable for a monetization vehicle that open weights would block, the claim rests on an asserted link rather than a demonstrated one. The evidence gap is that Side B conflates product integration (real) with commercial exclusivity derived from model closure (unproven).
